item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: Noting that this image is cc-compliant with the SCP wiki's license (CC BY-SA 3.0) as it originates from NASA and thus is Public Domain.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Intriguing concept, simple, and surprisingly, kind of haunting.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What on Neptune did I just read…..

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like that theory actually.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Sadly, the Foundation will get a probe out there and realize it's actually Voyager 2 that's trying to communicate with them…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Which stinks for me, because I preferred it being the photos. The words just showing up on the planet itself just seems too random for me. Unless it's a person on the planet doing it. Though there'd be no way of knowing without getting someone to view Neptune in person.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Probably because that's exactly what the first sentence of the description says - but then the article starts to imply heavily it's the planet itself before saying "Yea, let's contact the planet."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: For some reason, I got the impression that the text was showing up on the photographs them-self and not Neptune, and that the photographs of Neptune were what was possibly sapient.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Excellent. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's uncontained, possibly sapient, and is theoretically visible to anyone with a telescope of sufficient resolution (which is pretty much limited to governments and universiity-run observatories at this time, but that could change in the future).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Good job on the article. The only thing I don't get is why this is a Euclid? It's just text on top of pictures of Neptune. It's not like the text is going to escape and kill someone. +1 anyways.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I couldn't get past the silly, sorry but -1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Rewrite is definitely an improvement. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The concept is interesting, even worth keeping around, but I cannot upvote with such a glaring error:

All communications are presented in all capital letters, in a 96-point white sans serif font, and consist of fewer than 50 characters, typically taking the form of a brief question or request.

Points are a scale dating back to some of the earliest typesetting. The exact definition has varied throughout the years, finally stabilizing with the digital measurement system of 72 Points to one Imperial Inch. Even if the message is presented on top of the atmosphere, a 1-1/3" high message would not be picked up or be readable at even the highest resolution we can capture. "300m high" might be more accurate.

Technically, it would be a sans-serif 'typeface', not a 'font'. Font is formally a specific typeface in a specific size and weight. '30 point Times New Roman Bold' is a font; Verdana is a typeface (or family, if multiple weights are involved).

Also, "all capital" should be hyphenated, especially with the sentence beginning with "All".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I take it the new picture is far better than the old one; upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'd kinda like if it was the planet itself, just because of how sad it is to want someone around when it can't move to meet us and would destroy us if it did.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I liked the containment procedures more than the SCP itself.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like it, especially being an astronomy buff.

One thing - this thing has potential to be more-try to come up with some info about the creature sending the message, whether horrible or tragic, and add some [DATA EXPUNGED] to that. Put that in an addendum or something.

Just my two cents.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Same here, it need more of a hook than just a sad planet. Otherwise why would the immortal conciousness of Neptune be talking in English? Perhaps because it is the dead child of a NASA scientist who was into cybernetics and the occult?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: And I would really like to see it like in Voct's comment, as the actual concience of a comatose 13-year old from Earth. No vote for now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I just thought I'd get that out of the way.

And I agree. I'd love to see such a thing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Does it matter?
This'd be prime material for a tale, just waiting for someone clever.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I would like to state at this time that I was not aware of the existence of this article when writing SCP-1217. For that matter, I'm uncertain of which even came first.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Anyone notices the similarity between the messages here, and SCP-1217 ?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I agree. For some reason, I always liked the idea of sapient planets.

Not sure if I like the method of communication. It most certainly is quite weird, which is good for a SCP. But using or own alphabet and language might be a bit too much.

I'll upvote because it does give me the right feeling. But I feel like the image should be removed as soon as possible.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I actually like the idea that this is the same kind of entity as the Dickish Star, and that there's more than one of that sort of entity.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really like this one

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: How about, instead of having a planet write in English on any photograph taken of it, have it so that whoever looks at an "unaltered" photo sees the message in their native language? Maybe there's something memetic about the loneliest planet in our solar system that makes us see the messages. Or something.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Disregarding the LOLPlanet picture, I feel like this really works well. Even if the messages sent by the planet are overdone, they still work and they still make you sad. Or at least they make ME sad. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Completely by accident. The only thing I did to the image was superimpose the text. Maybe there really is someone trying to contact us from Neptune!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Huh. That… that strikingly resembles a human silhouette.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Here's a quick attempt that actually looks like what comes from the space probes.

And honestly, I'm not even all that crazy about this one.

You can see the full size version here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I MISS 7-ZARK-7

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
These letters in this don't look distorted, they just look like someone took a Smudge tool to them in Photoshop. As bad as the LOLPlanet image is, I think this one is actually worse.

Next, this doesn't look anything like the photos from Voyagers. This picture clearly suffers from excessive compression artifacts. Pictures transmitted from space probes aren't subject to lossy compression.

I would strongly recommend you go to NASA and actually look at what the raw images from the probes actually look like.

Here are some examples:

http://nssdc.gsfc.nasa.gov/imgcat/html/object_page/vg2_1127527.html
http://pds-rings.seti.org/browse/VG_0012/BROWSE/NEPTUNE/C1108XXX/

Most important is that _all_ space probe pictures are monochrome. Anything you see in published color is either a false-color image approximated with multiple exposures using different filters or an artistic interpretation (or as is usually the case, a bit of both).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think the main reason people downvote is that blisteringly awful picture.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don't know why this is downvoted, it seems a legit SCP to me, real mysterious like.
I will say the containment procedures are way too frickin' long, just put in something about belaying or editing any attempts by space programs to take detailed photos of neptune.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Something about that picture strikes me as a bit off, but it's much better than the current one and I'd be perfectly fine with it being used as a replacement photo.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Neat.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This I like.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Maybe a little something like this?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I still think I can improve this, but I've been on the fence as to how. A better picture would help, maybe having the message spelled out by the clouds rather than just appearing on it lolcats style. but I'm no good at photo editing myself. I've also considered having an interview log - say the satellite I mentioned already reached Neptune and burned out for some reason after a few hours, but before it did we had a brief conversation by beaming radio signals at the planet and photographing its responses.

I wanted to emphasize the sense of mystery when I rewrote it, but I think I may have gone too mysterious. I kind of liked the idea that Neptune itself is the source of the messages, and I've been thinking I could possibly tie it in with my own SCP-1688, the sentient lightning storm that's gotten "lost" on Earth. I'll think it over some more and attempt another rewrite tonight or tomorrow.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I can't decide what I think of this. Hm. It's not very good but there's something about it that speaks to me, enough that I don't want to downvote it.

Is there a rewrite still coming? It's at -10 as we speak…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: See I like the concept, and this could be really good if done well, just needs some improvement

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This, right here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: PLANETS HAVE FEELINGS T… wait, no they don't.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Reminds me of that Fact or Fiction series thingy hosted by Jonathan Frakes.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Eh… Though the idea potentially has merit, I can't upvote it. One nitpick that I have is that I fail to see any reason that the Foundation would -covertly-, of all things, launch a satellite. It would be far less resource-intensive to have the launch be aboveboard and just have its stated mission be monitoring insert science jargon on Neptune for insert science jargon reasons as opposed to making a space launch actually try and be stealthy.

What got my downvote, though, was the various things that it's said. "I'M SO LONELY" and variants thereof just feel rather ridiculously overdone, to me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"I'm a planet, I'm depressed, how do I communicate my feelings?"
*lightbulb/solarflare*
"I'LL SUPERIMPOSE WHITE AND CLEARLY LEGIBLE TEXT ON PICTURES OF ME!!!"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The current picture is full of fail.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
an intelligent entity

How do we know it's intelligent? It's not so much communicating as it is dumping random messages on us. It doesn't even consistently make coherent sentences.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I WANT MY MOM

I NEED TO FINISH MY HOMEWORK

PLEASE

I JUST WANNA GO HOME

I'M SO SORRY

I'LL NEVER DO IT AGAIN

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah, I'm really glad smapti came along and greatly improved my concept, so props to him. I hope we can all work together to make this a great SCP

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is such a fantastic idea. The only thing holding it back is the blandness of the actual messages, not to mention I feel like I've seen similar recently. There needs to be something more obvious going on beneath the surface.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I could rework the picture, so it doesn't look so "lolcat". I think the whole distorted font idea makes the concept more mysterious.

If you can confirm you are already not working on a picture I'll get right on it.

This was the old picture used in my first draft of this SCP -

Can you guys tell me if you think its better than the lolplanet one

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'll see if I can work on this some more again later tonight.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The letter S is what makes plurals. Not an apostrophe.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: "Com'on"? Where did I leave my skin peeler…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Tone is still off. I love the idea and feel as if "fleshing it out" would make it almost too clear. For me, personally, there's the right amount of vagueness going on. That's only me, personally, though, and there are areas where flesh can be added—delivery method, maybe make the messages less obvious, etc. This is really something that has potential, so I'd recommend the author keep a copy after deletion, rewrite, and repost.

For the tone…

A subsequent examination of NASA equipment confirmed that the communications were not being deliberately inserted into the photographs after reception and the information was present in the original radio transmissions from Voyager 2, and remote examination of Voyager 2's software indicated no means of introducing text on the part of the probe itself.

That's a run-on.

"several photos" should probably be "several photographs."

Neptune, and digitally edit

no comma.

have included;

should be "have included:"

and there are a bunch more that I'm too lazy to hunt out. The solution is to proof-read better, and maybe get someone else to check this over before submission.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I would like to see this concept succeed. The idea of a lonely consciousness of some sort connected to a distant object in the solar system has merit. And Smapti's rewrite has solved the tone issues. However, a couple of things still bug me.

- The picture. I don't know how to make the idea work in the way it's being described, so I apologize for not having a constructive alternative. But the "lolplanet" format is killing my suspension of disbelief.

- The messages are a little too straightforward for my taste. Maybe make them a little more esoteric or weird so that we don't immediately make the connection of "human-like consciousness stuck on Neptune and wants to go home".

I'm wondering if there's a different way to present the messages. Do they need to be texts accompanying images of the planet? What if it were a series of radio transmissions picked up by some soon-to-get-a-visit-from-some-guys-with-memory-drugs amateur astronomer, and then the Hubble or some probe picks up images of Neptune with some weird, anomalous formation in the atmosphere that can't be explained?

I'm going to upvote this to save it from deletion, but it needs something more to get it over the finish line. Honestly? I would like to see this work and somehow replace the Dickish Star.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Where is the Comic Sans when we really need it?
